Wikidata name: Metori no Himemiko
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. She was a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and [[:en:Princess Yata|Hata no Himemiko]].

== Biography ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.

The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying:
{{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}

Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.

Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.

The couple fled toward the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.

== Songs ==
Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang:
{{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}

== Analysis ==
1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds.
2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature.
3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms.
4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.

* {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55525858]]
Wikidata name: Metori no Himemiko
[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. She was a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and [[:en:Princess Yata|Hata no Himemiko]].

== Biography ==
According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.

The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying:
{{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}

Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.

Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.

The couple fled toward the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.

According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.

== Songs ==
Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang:
{{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}

== Analysis ==
1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds.
2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature.
3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms.
4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}}
* {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}}
* {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}}
* {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}

{{wikidata link|Q55525858}} [[Category:Characters in Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Executed Japanese people]] [[Category:Female Imperial Family Members before the Kofun Period]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Year of birth unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]

revision.comment: Add page with conflicting Wikidata QIDs
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55525858]] Wikidata name: Metori no Himemiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. She was a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and [[:en:Princess Yata|Hata no Himemiko]].
== Biography == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying: {{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}
Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.
Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.
The couple fled toward the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.
== Songs == Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang: {{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}
== Analysis == 1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds. 2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature. 3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms. 4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}} * {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}} * {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|Weaving|ja|機織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 織機|1=loom|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173056}} * {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Imperial-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|雌鳥皇女|version=88090097|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q55525858]] Wikidata name: Metori no Himemiko [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:08, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. She was a daughter of [[:en:Emperor Ōjin|Emperor Ōjin]], and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and [[:en:Princess Yata|Hata no Himemiko]].
== Biography == According to the [[:en:Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]], Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.
The [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]] states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying: {{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}
Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.
Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.
The couple fled toward the [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]], but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.
According to the [[:en:Kojiki|Kojiki]], the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.
== Songs == Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang: {{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}
== Analysis == 1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds. 2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature. 3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms. 4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}} * {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}} * {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}
== See Also == * [[:en:Ise Shrine|Ise Grand Shrine]] * {{ill|Weaving|ja|機織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 織機|1=loom|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173056}} * {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Imperial-stub}}
{{translated page|ja|雌鳥皇女|version=88090097|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:雌鳥皇女]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}} '''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. She was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and {{ill|Princess Yata|en|Princess Yata|WD=Q10892311|lt=Hata no Himemiko}}.
== Biography == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.
The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying: {{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}
Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.
Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.
The couple fled toward the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.
== Songs == Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang: {{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}
== Analysis == 1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds. 2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature. 3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms. 4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}} * {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}} * {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|Weaving|ja|機織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 織機|1=loom|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173056}} * {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Imperial-stub}}

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}}
'''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. She was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and {{ill|Princess Yata|en|Princess Yata|WD=Q10892311|lt=Hata no Himemiko}}.

== Biography ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.

The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying:
{{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}

Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.

Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.

The couple fled toward the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.

== Songs ==
Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang:
{{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}

== Analysis ==
1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds.
2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature.
3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms.
4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}}
* {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}}
* {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}}
* {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}

* {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311}}}} '''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}. She was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin}}, and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and {{ill|Princess Yata|en|Princess Yata|WD=Q10892311|lt=Hata no Himemiko}}.
== Biography == According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.
The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}} states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying: {{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}
Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.
Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.
The couple fled toward the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}}, but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.
According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki}}, the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.
== Songs == Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang: {{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}
== Analysis == 1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds. 2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature. 3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms. 4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.
== References == * {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}} * {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}} * {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}} * {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine}} * {{ill|Weaving|ja|機織|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weaving|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 織機|1=loom|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q173056}} * {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}
{{Japanese-history-stub}} {{Imperial-stub}}

{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989|1=Uji no Wakiiratsuko}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311|1=Princess Yata}}}}
'''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. She was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and {{ill|Princess Yata|en|Princess Yata|WD=Q10892311|lt=Hata no Himemiko|1=Princess Yata}}.

== Biography ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.

The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying:
{{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}

Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.

Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.

The couple fled toward the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Q52320308|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.

== Songs ==
Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang:
{{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}

== Analysis ==
1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds.
2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature.
3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms.
4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:雌鳥皇女]]
{{Infobox person|gender={{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}|father={{ill|Ōjin|WD=Q317997|1=Emperor Ōjin}}|relatives={{ill|Prince Uji no Wakiiratsuko|WD=Q11619989|1=Uji no Wakiiratsuko}}; {{ill|Yatanohimemiko|WD=Q10892311|1=Princess Yata}}}}
'''Metori no Himemiko''' (雌鳥皇女) was a Japanese imperial princess from the Kofun period (circa 5th century) mentioned in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} and {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. She was a daughter of {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|WD=Q317997|lt=Emperor Ōjin|1=Emperor Ōjin}}, and her mother was Miyanu no Yakahime (宮主宅媛 or 宮主矢河枝比売), the daughter of Hifure no Omi (日触使主) from the Wani no Omi clan. She was the younger sister of {{ill|Uji no Wakiratsuko|ja|菟道稚郎子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Uji no Wakiratsuko|1=Uji no Wakiiratsuko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11619989}} and {{ill|Princess Yata|en|Princess Yata|WD=Q10892311|lt=Hata no Himemiko|1=Princess Yata}}.

== Biography ==
According to the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|WD=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, Emperor Nintoku attempted to marry his half-sister Metori no Himemiko, who was also the younger sister of his wife, Hata no Himemiko. The intermediary chosen for this task was their half-brother, {{ill|Hayabusa Wake no Miko|ja|隼別皇子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hayabusa Wake no Miko|1=Prince Hayabusawake|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4378819}}. However, Hayabusa Wake secretly took Metori no Himemiko as his own wife, defying the emperor's command.

The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} states that Metori no Himemiko, fearing the jealousy of Emperor Nintoku's empress, volunteered to marry Hayabusa Wake, saying:
{{quotation|Due to the empress's strong temper, His Majesty has not taken Hata no Wakiratsume as his wife. Therefore, I will not serve him either. I will become your wife instead. (translated by Yasuo Hagiwara)<ref name="Metori1">{{ill|Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja|コンサイス日本人名辞典 改訂新版|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Concise Dictionary of Japanese Names (Revised Edition)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Sanseido, 1993, pp. 1244–1245.</ref>}}

Unaware of the situation, Emperor Nintoku went to visit Metori no Himemiko, but upon hearing songs from her attendants, he learned the truth and initially forgave them, considering their feelings and his familial bond with Hayabusa Wake.

Later, Metori no Himemiko and Hayabusa Wake were overheard comparing themselves to Emperor Nintoku, implying that Hayabusa Wake was superior in attractiveness and capability. The emperor became furious, believing this to be an act of rebellion, and ordered their capture.

The couple fled toward the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}, but Emperor Nintoku interpreted this as an escape attempt. He dispatched soldiers led by {{ill|Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|ja|吉備品遅部雄鯽|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Honochi no Ofuna|1=Q52320308|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52320308}} and {{ill|Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja|播磨佐伯直阿俄能胡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Harima Saeki no Atae Aganoko|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to pursue them.

According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|WD=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, the couple was ultimately killed at Komoshiro no no in Ise Province (蘇邇 in Uda according to the Kojiki). Emperor Nintoku's commanders found jewelry in the possession of Metori no Himemiko, which led to further disputes.

== Songs ==
Both the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki preserve ancient songs attributed to Metori no Himemiko, showcasing her wit and resolve. For example, in the Kojiki, she sang:
{{quotation|The skylark flies high in the sky, but swifter is Hayabusa Wake, who surpasses the wren.}}

== Analysis ==
1. The names of the characters are all associated with birds.
2. The story is structured as a song-based narrative, characteristic of ancient Japanese literature.
3. Metori no Himemiko is portrayed as a proactive and independent woman, challenging social norms.
4. The treatment of her remains reflects the taboo against desecrating the dead, though such practices were not uncommon.

== References ==
* {{Cite book|title=Kojiki|publisher=Shogakukan|date=1983}}
* {{Cite book|title=Nihon Shoki (Volume 2)|publisher=Iwanami Bunko|date=1994}}
* {{Cite book|title=Complete Modern Translation of Nihon Shoki (Volume 1)|publisher=Kodansha Gakujutsu Bunko|author=Mamoru Ujitani|date=1988}}
* {{Cite book|title=Japanese Ancient Clan Name Dictionary|publisher=Yoshikawa Kobunkan|supervisor=Saburo Sakamoto|date=1990}}


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}

* {{ill|Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|ja|舎人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Attendant (Japanese imperial court)|1=Retainer|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q126081127}}
